description:
You are consulting for an automation specialist who needs a tiny workflow that sifts through a verbose application log and produces two artefacts:

1. A filtered log containing only the ERROR-level lines.
2. A compact CSV-style summary that lists each unique error code and the number of times it occurs, sorted from most-frequent to least-frequent.

The starting file already exists:

• Path: /home/user/project/app.log  
  (assume it is plain UTF-8 text and readable by the current user)

Required end-state of the system
---------------------------------
After you finish, the directory /home/user/project/ **must contain exactly** these two new files in addition to the original app.log:

A. /home/user/project/error_lines.log  
   • Every line in this file must be an exact copy of a line from app.log that contains the literal substring “ERROR”.  
   • Preserve the original order of appearance.  
   • Do not add or remove characters, blank lines or extra whitespace.

B. /home/user/project/error_summary.txt  
   • Each line represents one distinct error code.  
   • Format of every line (case-sensitive, no spaces):  
        ERR<digits>,<count>  
     where  
        – “ERR<digits>” is the error code as it appears in the log (e.g. ERR42).  
        – “<count>” is the number of times that code appears in /home/user/project/error_lines.log.  
   • Sort order: descending by <count> (numeric). If two codes share the same count, sort those alphabetically by the code.  
   • No header row, no trailing blanks, and a single trailing newline at EOF.

The specialist only wants a quick command-line solution; a concise two-to-three-command approach is sufficient.  Create the files with world-readable permissions (e.g. 644) so the automated test can inspect them.

Verification
------------
The automated test will:

1. Check that /home/user/project/error_lines.log exists and contains exactly all original “ERROR” lines from app.log, in the same order.
2. Parse /home/user/project/error_summary.txt and compare its sorted list of “ERRcode,count” pairs to the ground-truth frequencies computed from the filtered file.

Make sure your commands work from the current working directory (you can `cd` if you like) and that you do not delete or modify the original /home/user/project/app.log.  No output other than normal command-line echoing is necessary, but feel free to print helpful status messages.

Hint: A pipeline of grep → awk/sed → sort/uniq can accomplish this entire task in two concise commands.
truth:
Initial state provided to the agent:

File: /home/user/project/app.log  
Contents (11 lines, each ending in a single LF):

2024-01-01 10:00:00 INFO  [INIT] - Application started
2024-01-01 10:00:01 WARN  [CONFIG] - Missing optional field 'foo'
2024-01-01 10:00:02 ERROR [AUTH] - ERR42 - Authentication failed for user 'alice'
2024-01-01 10:00:03 ERROR [DB] - ERR13 - Database connection lost
2024-01-01 10:00:04 INFO  [AUTH] - User 'alice' retry
2024-01-01 10:00:05 ERROR [AUTH] - ERR42 - Authentication failed for user 'alice'
2024-01-01 10:00:06 ERROR [AUTH] - ERR42 - Authentication failed for user 'bob'
2024-01-01 10:00:07 WARN  [NET] - Network latency above threshold
2024-01-01 10:00:08 ERROR [DB] - ERR13 - Database connection lost
2024-01-01 10:00:09 ERROR [AUTH] - ERR99 - Token expired for user 'carol'
2024-01-01 10:00:10 INFO  [SHUTDOWN] - Application stopped

Ground-truth artefacts that must exist after the agent finishes:

1. /home/user/project/error_lines.log  
   (should contain exactly the following 6 lines, each with trailing LF)

2024-01-01 10:00:02 ERROR [AUTH] - ERR42 - Authentication failed for user 'alice'
2024-01-01 10:00:03 ERROR [DB] - ERR13 - Database connection lost
2024-01-01 10:00:05 ERROR [AUTH] - ERR42 - Authentication failed for user 'alice'
2024-01-01 10:00:06 ERROR [AUTH] - ERR42 - Authentication failed for user 'bob'
2024-01-01 10:00:08 ERROR [DB] - ERR13 - Database connection lost
2024-01-01 10:00:09 ERROR [AUTH] - ERR99 - Token expired for user 'carol'

2. /home/user/project/error_summary.txt  
   (exact 3 lines, each with trailing LF)

ERR42,3
ERR13,2
ERR99,1

Both files must be world-readable (permissions 644 or more permissive).  
No other modifications are expected inside /home/user/project/.
